title: Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC
abstract: arXiv:2606.27286v1 Announce Type: new  Abstract: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterio

Title: Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC
Abstract: arXiv:2606.27286v1 Announce Type: new  Abstract: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterio
translated_title: 基于模拟的推理在流行病学模型中快速贝叶斯参数估计的应用：与MCMC的比较
translated_abstract: 机理流行病学模型广泛应用于支持传染病预测和公共卫生决策。这类模型的贝叶斯校准通常使用马尔可夫链蒙特卡洛（MCMC）方法，但对于高维非线性系统和重复的近实时分析，MCMC可能变得计算成本高昂。本文研究了基于模拟的推理（SBI）结合神经后验估计，作为一种可扩展的替代方案，用于对机理SECIR流行病学模型进行贝叶斯校准，并使用了2020年德国COVID-19重症监护病房（ICU）占用数据。我们比较了SBI和MCMC在多个疫情阶段的表现，分别使用了31天推断窗口和更具挑战性的涉及多个传播变化点的201天重建问题。通过Wasserstein距离和Kullback-Leibler散度以及后验分布对后验一致性进行了定量评估。
tldr: 本文提出使用基于模拟的推理（SBI）结合神经后验估计，作为MCMC的快速替代方案，用于流行病学模型的贝叶斯校准，并在COVID-19 ICU数据上验证了其计算效率和后验准确性。
en_tdlr: This paper proposes simulation-based inference (SBI) with neural posterior estimation as a fast alternative to MCMC for Bayesian calibration of epidemiological models, demonstrating its computational efficiency and posterior accuracy on COVID-19 ICU data.
